parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I tried to shake it off today, but it didn't work. I just feel wound up and on edge.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really uncomfortable. When you’re wound up like that, even small things can feel bigger and harder to deal with.

If you want, we can try to lower the intensity a little right now:
- Take one slow breath in for 4, out for 6, a few times
- Unclench your jaw and drop your shoulders
- Name 5 things you can see, 4 you can feel, 3 you can hear

If you’d rather talk it through, I’m here. What do you think set it off today?

turns.user_message: My chest gets tight every time I try to relax lately. I can't remember the last time I felt truly calm.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really uncomfortable and exhausting. When your body tightens up just as you’re trying to relax, it can feel like you never get a real break.

A few things to keep in mind:
- Chest tightness can happen with anxiety or stress, but it’s also something to take seriously if it’s new, severe, or happening with symptoms like shortness of breath, dizziness, sweating, pain spreading to your arm/jaw/back, or if you have heart/lung concerns. If any of that is happening, please seek urgent medical care.
- If this feels more like stress/anxiety, your body may be staying in “alert mode” even when you want to rest.

For right now, you could try a very small reset:
1. Unclench your jaw and drop your shoulders.
2. Put one hand on your chest and one on your belly.
3. Breathe out slowly a little longer than you breathe in, for 1–2 minutes.
4. Name 5 things you can see around you to help ground yourself.

Also, if you haven’t had a checkup about this chest tightness, it could be worth talking to a doctor or clinician, just to rule out physical causes and get peace of mind.

If you want, you can tell me what tends to happen right before the tightness starts—I can help you sort through whether it feels more like anxiety, stress, or something else.

turns.user_message: I feel like I'm constantly braced for something awful to happen. It's exhausting to live with this much tension all the time.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really exhausting. Living like you’re always waiting for the other shoe to drop can drain so much energy.

You don’t have to handle that feeling alone. If you want, we can slow it down together and figure out what it’s like for you:
- When do you notice the tension most?
- Does it feel more like worry, dread, physical tightness, or being on alert?
- Has anything been happening lately that might be making your body stay in “ready for danger” mode?

For right now, you might try something small and concrete:
- Unclench your jaw and drop your shoulders
- Exhale longer than you inhale a few times
- Name 5 things you can see around you
- Remind yourself: “I’m here, and this moment is not the thing I’m afraid of”

If this has been going on a lot or is getting in the way of sleep, work, or feeling okay day to day, it could really help to talk with a mental health professional. And if you ever start feeling like you might hurt yourself or you’re not safe, please call emergency services or a crisis line right away.

If you want, tell me what the tension feels like in your body or what tends to set it off, and I’ll stay with you through it.
